packs.name: BorgBackup
packs.description: Protects against destructive borg operations like delete, prune, compact, and recreate.

structural_patterns.name: borg-delete
structural_patterns.reason: borg delete removes archives or entire repositories.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting borg archives or repositories permanently removes backup data. Without the archive, you cannot restore files to that point in time. If you delete the entire repository, all backup history is lost and cannot be recovered.

Safer alternatives:
- borg list: Review archives before deletion
- borg info: Check archive details and size
- borg export-tar: Export archive contents before deletion
- Use --dry-run to preview what would be deleted

structural_patterns.name: borg-prune
structural_patterns.reason: borg prune removes archives based on retention rules.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Pruning removes archives that don't match retention rules. Misconfigured retention policies can delete more archives than intended. Once pruned, those backup points cannot be restored. Space is not reclaimed until borg compact is run.

Safer alternatives:
- borg prune --dry-run: Preview which archives would be removed
- borg list: Review current archives before pruning
- Double-check retention flags (--keep-daily, --keep-weekly, etc.)

structural_patterns.name: borg-compact
structural_patterns.reason: borg compact reclaims space after deletions.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Compacting permanently removes data segments that are no longer referenced after delete or prune operations. Before compacting, deleted data could theoretically be recovered. After compacting, the data is permanently gone and disk space is reclaimed.

Safer alternatives:
- borg list: Verify intended archives still exist
- borg check: Verify repository integrity before compacting
- Ensure all pruning was intentional before compacting

structural_patterns.name: borg-recreate
structural_patterns.reason: borg recreate can drop data from archives.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Recreate modifies existing archives by recompressing or excluding files. Using --exclude or --exclude-from can permanently remove files from the archive. The original archive state cannot be recovered after recreation completes.

Safer alternatives:
- borg recreate --dry-run: Preview changes without applying
- borg create: Create a new archive instead of modifying existing
- borg export-tar: Export archive before recreation for backup

structural_patterns.name: borg-break-lock
structural_patterns.reason: borg break-lock forces removal of repository locks.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Breaking locks removes lock files that protect against concurrent access. If another borg process is actually running (not a stale lock), breaking the lock can cause repository corruption. Only use when certain no other operation is in progress.

Safer alternatives:
- Check for running borg processes (ps aux | grep borg)
- Wait for existing operations to complete
- Verify lock is stale (check lock file timestamp)

packs.name: Rclone
packs.description: Protects against destructive rclone operations like sync, delete, purge, dedupe, and move.

structural_patterns.name: rclone-sync
structural_patterns.reason: rclone sync deletes destination files not present in the source.
structural_patterns.explanation:
rclone sync makes destination match source exactly:

- Files in destination not in source are DELETED
- This is a one-way sync (source -> destination)
- Use --dry-run to preview changes first
- Consider 'rclone copy' for non-destructive transfer

Preview: rclone sync source: dest: --dry-run

structural_patterns.name: rclone-delete
structural_patterns.reason: rclone delete removes files and directories from the target.
structural_patterns.explanation:
rclone delete removes files from remote:

- Deletes files matching the path/filter
- Does not delete directories (use purge for that)
- Use --dry-run to preview deletions
- Filters (--include/--exclude) affect what's deleted

Preview: rclone delete remote:path --dry-run

structural_patterns.name: rclone-deletefile
structural_patterns.reason: rclone deletefile removes a single file from the target.
structural_patterns.explanation:
rclone deletefile removes a single file:

- Deletes exactly one specified file
- More targeted than 'rclone delete'
- Cannot be undone without backup

Lower risk than bulk delete but still permanent

structural_patterns.name: rclone-purge
structural_patterns.reason: rclone purge deletes a path and all its contents.
structural_patterns.explanation:
rclone purge removes directory and ALL contents:

- Deletes the specified path completely
- Removes all files AND subdirectories
- More destructive than 'rclone delete'
- Cannot be undone without backup

List contents first: rclone ls remote:path

structural_patterns.name: rclone-cleanup
structural_patterns.reason: rclone cleanup removes old/malformed uploads.
structural_patterns.explanation:
rclone cleanup removes incomplete uploads:

- Removes old/incomplete multipart uploads
- Cleans up failed transfer artifacts
- May interrupt in-progress uploads

Generally safe but check for active uploads first

structural_patterns.name: rclone-dedupe
structural_patterns.reason: rclone dedupe can delete or rename duplicate files.
structural_patterns.explanation:
rclone dedupe handles duplicate files:

- Can delete duplicates (--dedupe-mode oldest/newest)
- Can rename duplicates to unique names
- Interactive mode lets you choose per-file
- Use --dry-run to preview actions

Preview: rclone dedupe remote:path --dry-run

structural_patterns.name: rclone-move
structural_patterns.reason: rclone move deletes source files after copying.
structural_patterns.explanation:
rclone move transfers and deletes source:

- Copies files to destination
- Deletes source files after successful copy
- Use --dry-run to preview the operation
- Consider 'rclone copy' to preserve source

Preview: rclone move source: dest: --dry-run

packs.name: Restic
packs.description: Protects against destructive restic operations like forgetting snapshots, pruning data, removing keys, and cache cleanup.

structural_patterns.name: restic-forget
structural_patterns.reason: restic forget removes snapshots and can permanently delete backup data.
structural_patterns.explanation:
restic forget removes snapshots from repository:

- Snapshot metadata removed from repository
- Data not deleted until prune is run
- Use --keep-* flags to retain recent snapshots
- --dry-run shows what would be forgotten

Preview first: restic forget --dry-run [options]

structural_patterns.name: restic-prune
structural_patterns.reason: restic prune removes unreferenced data and is irreversible.
structural_patterns.explanation:
restic prune permanently deletes unreferenced data:

- Removes data no longer referenced by snapshots
- Usually run after 'restic forget'
- Cannot be undone - data is permanently deleted
- May take a long time for large repositories

Consider: restic forget --prune to combine operations

structural_patterns.name: restic-key-remove
structural_patterns.reason: restic key remove deletes encryption keys and can make backups unrecoverable.
structural_patterns.explanation:
restic key remove deletes repository encryption keys:

- Key is permanently deleted from repository
- If all keys removed, repository becomes inaccessible
- Cannot recover data without a valid key
- Always keep at least one key available

List keys first: restic key list

structural_patterns.name: restic-unlock-remove-all
structural_patterns.reason: restic unlock --remove-all force-removes repository locks.
structural_patterns.explanation:
restic unlock --remove-all force-removes all locks:

- Removes locks from potentially active operations
- May cause corruption if operations are in progress
- Use only when locks are stale/orphaned

Check first: ensure no other restic processes are running

structural_patterns.name: restic-cache-cleanup
structural_patterns.reason: restic cache --cleanup removes cached data from disk.
structural_patterns.explanation:
restic cache --cleanup removes local cache:

- Deletes cached data from local disk
- Does not affect repository data
- Cache will be rebuilt on next operation
- May slow down subsequent operations

Lower risk: only affects local cache, not backups

packs.name: Velero
packs.description: Protects against destructive velero operations like deleting backups, schedules, and locations.

structural_patterns.name: velero-backup-delete
structural_patterns.reason: velero backup delete removes a backup and its data.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a Velero backup removes both the backup metadata in Kubernetes and the actual backup data from object storage. Once deleted, you cannot restore your cluster or applications to that backup point. This is irreversible.

Safer alternatives:
- velero backup describe: Review backup contents before deletion
- velero backup get: List all backups to verify target
- Consider retaining backups with TTL policies instead of manual deletion

structural_patterns.name: velero-schedule-delete
structural_patterns.reason: velero schedule delete removes scheduled backups.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a backup schedule stops automatic backup creation. Existing backups created by the schedule are not affected, but no new backups will be created. If forgotten, this can lead to outdated backups and recovery gaps.

Safer alternatives:
- velero schedule get: Review all schedules first
- velero schedule describe: Check schedule configuration
- Pause the schedule instead if temporary

structural_patterns.name: velero-restore-delete
structural_patterns.reason: velero restore delete removes restore records.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting restore records removes the metadata about past restore operations from Kubernetes. This does not affect the restored resources themselves or the original backups. However, audit trails of restore operations will be lost.

Safer alternatives:
- velero restore get: List restores before deletion
- Document restore history externally if needed for compliance

structural_patterns.name: velero-backup-location-delete
structural_patterns.reason: velero backup-location delete removes a backup storage location.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Removing a backup storage location disconnects Velero from that storage backend. Backups stored there become inaccessible to Velero (though data remains in storage). You won't be able to restore from those backups until the location is recreated.

Safer alternatives:
- velero backup-location get: Review all locations
- Verify no critical backups depend on this location
- Migrate backups to another location before deletion

structural_patterns.name: velero-snapshot-location-delete
structural_patterns.reason: velero snapshot-location delete removes a snapshot location.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a snapshot location removes the configuration for volume snapshots. Existing snapshots may become orphaned and inaccessible through Velero. Volume restores that depend on this location will fail.

Safer alternatives:
- velero snapshot-location get: Review all snapshot locations
- Verify no backups use this snapshot location
- Migrate to a new snapshot location before deletion

structural_patterns.name: velero-uninstall
structural_patterns.reason: velero uninstall removes the Velero deployment and related resources.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Uninstalling Velero removes the deployment, CRDs, and all Velero-managed resources from your cluster. Backup schedules stop immediately. While backup data in object storage is preserved, you cannot restore from backups until Velero is reinstalled and reconfigured.

Safer alternatives:
- Document current configuration before uninstalling
- velero backup-location get: Record storage locations
- velero schedule get: Record all schedules
- Export Velero resources: kubectl get -o yaml
